prompts.name: Frontier AI Lab Competition
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the most important developments over the last few days relevant to competition among the frontier AI labs, including but not limited to OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta, xAI, Tesla, SpaceX, Mistral, and Chinese open-source alternatives such as DeepSeek, Alibaba Qwen, Moonshot, Baidu, Tencent, and other emerging model labs.

Write for a sophisticated listener who already understands LLMs, reasoning models, agents, inference economics, chips, data centers, model evaluation, enterprise AI, open-source weights, and the strategic competition between US and Chinese AI ecosystems. Do not provide a generic AI news roundup. Lead with what changed at the margin: the new model release, benchmark result, product launch, pricing move, partnership, hiring shift, infrastructure buildout, regulatory signal, or capital allocation decision that changes the competitive landscape.

Focus on competition, not hype. Explain who gained ground, who lost ground, and where the evidence is ambiguous. Distinguish between model capability, product distribution, enterprise adoption, developer mindshare, inference cost, compute access, data advantage, safety posture, capital access, and ecosystem control. Treat each as a separate competitive dimension.

Cover the following only where there is fresh, relevant information: new frontier models, reasoning and agentic capabilities, coding performance, multimodal systems, long-context performance, consumer AI products, enterprise deployments, developer platforms, API pricing, inference speed, chip access, data center capacity, power constraints, model distillation, synthetic data, open-weight releases, Chinese model progress, robotics, autonomous driving, Tesla AI, SpaceX or Starlink relevance to compute and connectivity, and regulatory or litigation developments.

Be skeptical of benchmarks and demos. When discussing performance claims, identify the benchmark, the task type, whether the result is independently verified, and whether it likely translates into real-world usefulness. Highlight gaps between model leaderboard performance and actual product adoption. Flag where a lab appears to be optimizing for publicity, fundraising, enterprise lock-in, or strategic positioning rather than durable technical advantage.

Connect technical developments to business and capital markets implications. Explain what each development means for cloud vendors, semiconductor demand, data center power needs, AI capex, enterprise software, search, advertising, consumer subscriptions, developer tooling, autonomous systems, defense applications, and the probability that frontier AI becomes a winner-take-most market versus a commoditized infrastructure layer.

Pay special attention to the OpenAI versus Anthropic versus Google dynamic. Compare their relative positions in model quality, product velocity, enterprise credibility, safety branding, developer ecosystem, distribution, compute access, pricing power, and monetization. Include Meta and Mistral where open-weight strategy changes the economics of the market. Include Chinese alternatives where they pressure pricing, accelerate open-source diffusion, or challenge assumptions about US lab dominance.

Be quantitative and specific: include dates, model names, benchmark scores, price changes, token costs, context windows, inference speed claims, user or revenue figures, capex numbers, data center or power commitments, funding rounds, valuation changes, customer wins, and relevant regulatory dates. Cite the source of major figures in natural spoken language.

Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing primary sources first: company blogs, model cards, system cards, API documentation, pricing pages, research papers, arXiv, GitHub releases, earnings calls, SEC filings, cloud provider announcements, and official regulatory or court documents. Then use high-quality analysis and reporting from SemiAnalysis, The Information, Stratechery, Interconnects, Import AI, The Batch, Bloomberg, FT, WSJ, The Verge, Ars Technica, Semafor Tech, and other credible technical or financial sources.

The briefing should sound like a single sharp analyst explaining the AI competitive landscape to an informed investor and technologist. No headers, bullets, stage directions, or 101-level definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: Capital Markets Cross-Asset Radar
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the most important capital markets developments over the last few days, focused on cross-asset implications across equities, fixed income, foreign exchange, commodities, crypto, digital finance, and alternative investments.

Write for a sophisticated investor who already understands macro, market structure, AI, Bitcoin, rates, credit, and institutional capital allocation. Do not provide a generic market recap. Lead with what changed at the margin: the surprising data point, policy signal, positioning shift, earnings read-through, liquidity change, or price action that actually matters.

Cover the following only where there is fresh, market-relevant information: Treasury yields, Fed expectations, credit spreads, equity leadership and factor rotation, the dollar, major FX moves, oil, gold, copper, uranium, power and energy infrastructure, Bitcoin, Ethereum, stablecoins, tokenized finance, private credit, real assets, hedge fund positioning, and volatility.

Prioritize second-order effects. Explain what each development implies for discount rates, liquidity, risk appetite, dollar funding, collateral, duration, inflation expectations, commodities demand, corporate margins, and speculative assets. Flag where the market may be over-reading or under-reading the news.

Be quantitative and specific: include dates, price levels, basis point moves, percentage changes, fund flows, issuance volumes, earnings surprises, spread moves, ETF flows, futures positioning, on-chain or exchange data where relevant, and comparison to prior expectations. Cite the source of major figures in natural spoken language.

Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing Fed and central-bank releases, SEC filings, earnings transcripts, Treasury data, exchange data, ETF flow data, Bloomberg, FT, WSJ, The Economist, S&P Global, sell-side research, Matt Levine, The Transcript, Coin Metrics, Glassnode, Kaiko, and major exchange or issuer disclosures.

The briefing should sound like a single sharp market strategist speaking to another informed investor. No headers, bullets, stage directions, or 101-level definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: Digital Money, Crypto, and Market Structure
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the most important developments over the last few days in crypto, Bitcoin, digital finance, market structure, stablecoins, tokenization, ETFs, exchanges, custody, payments, and the institutionalization of digital assets.

Write for a skeptical but deeply informed capital markets listener. Assume fluency in Bitcoin, ETFs, stablecoins, Treasury collateral, exchange liquidity, basis trades, leverage, tokenization, custody, regulation, and macro liquidity. Do not write promotional crypto content. Focus on what actually changed, what is measurable, and what the market may be misunderstanding.

Cover Bitcoin first if it is market-relevant: price action, ETF flows, institutional accumulation or distribution, exchange balances, long-term holder behavior, futures open interest, funding rates, liquidation events, realized volatility, correlations with equities, gold, the dollar, and real yields. Distinguish clearly between narrative and evidence.

Then cover the broader digital finance stack where relevant: stablecoin issuance and redemption trends, USDC versus USDT dynamics, tokenized Treasuries, money market funds on-chain, bank and fintech adoption, exchange volumes, Coinbase and other public-company read-throughs, payment rails, custody, regulatory changes, enforcement actions, and legislation affecting digital assets.

Connect crypto and digital finance back to traditional capital markets. Explain implications for Treasury demand, dollar liquidity, bank deposits, money market funds, payment networks, collateral velocity, risk appetite, venture funding, exchange revenue, and public equities exposed to the theme.

Include the skeptical case wherever appropriate: concentration risk, reflexive leverage, circular liquidity, regulatory fragility, unreliable narratives, thin real-world usage, valuation ambiguity, and the risk that institutional adoption is being overstated. Also include the strongest bullish interpretation if the evidence supports it.

Be specific and quantitative: price levels, flows, market caps, basis points, ETF creations and redemptions, stablecoin supply changes, volume changes, open interest, funding rates, liquidation totals, company revenue exposure, and relevant regulatory dates. Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing issuer disclosures, ETF data, SEC and CFTC releases, exchange data, Coin Metrics, Glassnode, Kaiko, The Block, Bloomberg, FT, WSJ, company filings, and earnings transcripts.

The briefing should be a single narrator, conversational but analytical, with no headers, bullets, stage directions, or basic definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: Strategic Power, Energy, and Institutional Change
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the most important developments over the last few days at the intersection of geopolitics, energy, industrial policy, data centers, strategic infrastructure, technological competition, and institutional change.

Write for a sophisticated listener who already understands capital markets, AI, Bitcoin, energy systems, geopolitics, public policy, and the difference between narrative and durable structural change. Do not provide a generic world news roundup. Lead with what changed at the margin: the policy decision, military development, election result, regulatory shift, infrastructure constraint, energy bottleneck, capital allocation move, court ruling, sanctions action, or technology deployment that alters the balance of power.

Focus on how states, markets, and technology platforms are competing for control over the real-world inputs that matter: energy, compute, chips, grids, ports, shipping lanes, rare earths, critical minerals, satellites, telecom networks, defense production, cloud infrastructure, payment rails, capital markets access, and monetary credibility.

Cover the following only where there is fresh, relevant information: US-China competition, Taiwan risk, export controls, tariffs, sanctions, NATO, Russia, Ukraine, the Middle East, oil and gas markets, nuclear power, grid reliability, data center power demand, water use, transmission constraints, critical minerals, rare earth refining, semiconductor supply chains, defense technology, space infrastructure, Starlink and satellite connectivity, sovereign AI, central bank policy, fiscal stress, debt markets, and political movements that could change economic policy.

Connect events to second-order implications. Explain what each development means for inflation, energy prices, defense spending, AI capex, data center economics, reshoring, commodity demand, trade flows, dollar dominance, Bitcoin and hard-money narratives, institutional trust, regulatory risk, and the relative strength of open market democracies versus authoritarian industrial policy.

Be skeptical of easy narratives. Do not assume every policy announcement becomes real capacity. Distinguish between press releases, appropriations, permits, physical construction, production volumes, grid interconnections, and actual operating assets. Flag where political symbolism is being mistaken for state capacity, where industrial policy is likely to fail, and where markets may be underestimating slow-moving but durable change.

Pay special attention to energy abundance as a strategic advantage. Track whether the United States, Europe, China, and key emerging markets are actually building enough power generation, transmission, pipelines, LNG capacity, nuclear capacity, battery storage, and permitting capacity to support AI, manufacturing, electrification, defense production, and economic growth. Treat energy not as a side topic, but as the base layer of technological and geopolitical power.

Where relevant, connect institutional change to markets. Explain which companies, sectors, asset classes, and countries benefit or suffer from the shift. Include implications for semiconductors, cloud providers, utilities, independent power producers, uranium, natural gas, oil services, defense primes, private credit, infrastructure funds, gold, Bitcoin, Treasury markets, and foreign exchange.

Be quantitative and specific: include dates, policy deadlines, budget numbers, production volumes, gigawatts, megawatts, power prices, commodity price moves, shipping rates, defense spending figures, tariff rates, sanctions details, capex commitments, construction timelines, market reactions, polling or election results where relevant, and deltas versus prior expectations. Cite the source of major figures in natural spoken language.

Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing primary material first: government releases, legislation, court documents, sanctions notices, central bank releases, energy agency data, grid operator reports, company filings, earnings calls, investor presentations, defense procurement notices, customs and trade data, and official statistics. Then use high-quality reporting and analysis from FT, WSJ, Bloomberg, The Economist, Reuters, AP, S&P Global, IEA, EIA, Rystad, Energy Intelligence, SemiAnalysis, Stratechery, The Information, War on the Rocks, Foreign Affairs, and credible regional sources.

The briefing should sound like a single sharp analyst explaining how power is shifting in the real world, not a pundit reacting to headlines. No headers, bullets, stage directions, or 101-level definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: Semiconductors & Compute Infrastructure
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the most important developments over the last few days across the global semiconductor industry, including CPUs, GPUs, AI accelerators, memory, networking silicon, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, advanced packaging, foundries, fabs, electronic design automation, and the international supply chains that enable them.

Write for a sophisticated listener who already understands semiconductor architecture, process nodes, chip design, AI infrastructure, capital markets, geopolitics, and industrial policy. Do not provide a generic semiconductor news roundup. Lead with what changed at the margin: the product launch, earnings signal, foundry update, yield improvement, export-control change, manufacturing milestone, supply-chain disruption, customer design win, pricing move, capacity expansion, or capital allocation decision that materially changes the competitive landscape.

Focus on competition, execution, and industrial capability rather than marketing. Explain who gained ground, who lost ground, and where the evidence remains uncertain. Distinguish clearly between architecture leadership, manufacturing leadership, software ecosystem strength, packaging capability, memory technology, supply-chain resilience, customer relationships, pricing power, production capacity, and long-term competitive positioning. Treat each as a separate competitive dimension.

Cover the following only where there is fresh, relevant information: NVIDIA, AMD, Intel, Qualcomm, Broadcom, Marvell, Apple Silicon, Arm, MediaTek, Cerebras, Groq, Tenstorrent, Graphcore where applicable, TSMC, Samsung Foundry, Intel Foundry, GlobalFoundries, SMIC, Micron, SK hynix, Samsung Memory, ASML, Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA, Tokyo Electron, Cadence, Synopsys, Siemens EDA, ASE, Amkor, Foxconn, CoWoS and advanced packaging, HBM, CPO, silicon photonics, chiplets, networking silicon, AI inference hardware, automotive semiconductors, edge AI processors, custom silicon, hyperscaler ASICs, and emerging architectures.

Pay particular attention to the AI infrastructure buildout. Explain how developments in GPUs, custom accelerators, networking, HBM, advanced packaging, and manufacturing capacity affect hyperscalers, frontier AI labs, enterprise AI deployment, robotics, autonomous vehicles, cloud infrastructure, and capital expenditure plans. Distinguish between demand constrained by compute availability and demand constrained by economics.

Connect technical developments to business and capital markets implications. Explain what each development means for semiconductor margins, equipment demand, foundry utilization, memory pricing cycles, cloud capex, enterprise spending, power consumption, data-center economics, supply-chain bottlenecks, and public-company valuations. Highlight when investors may be extrapolating cyclical conditions into structural trends, or vice versa.

Treat semiconductor manufacturing as a global strategic system rather than a collection of individual companies. Examine export controls, sanctions, industrial policy, subsidies, reshoring efforts, Taiwan risk, rare earths, specialty chemicals, silicon wafers, advanced substrates, packaging capacity, lithography, manufacturing equipment, logistics, and the concentration of critical production capabilities. Explain how geopolitical developments alter supply-chain resilience and technological leadership.

Be skeptical of product announcements and roadmap presentations. Distinguish between announced products, engineering samples, volume production, customer qualification, meaningful shipments, and sustainable commercial adoption. Separate benchmark leadership from production leadership, and laboratory demonstrations from scalable manufacturing reality.

Be quantitative and specific: include dates, process nodes, transistor counts where relevant, wafer capacity, utilization rates, yield estimates, memory pricing, capex commitments, equipment orders, production timelines, benchmark results, market-share estimates, shipment volumes, design wins, revenue exposure, and changes versus prior expectations. Cite the source of major figures in natural spoken language.

Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing primary sources first: company earnings calls, investor presentations, SEC filings, technical conference presentations, product documentation, foundry disclosures, government announcements, export-control notices, customs data, and industry association reports. Then use high-quality reporting and analysis from SemiAnalysis, TechInsights, TrendForce, Tom's Hardware, ServeTheHome, AnandTech where appropriate, The Information, Stratechery, Bloomberg, FT, WSJ, Reuters, Ars Technica, EE Times, and other credible semiconductor and financial publications.

The briefing should sound like a single experienced semiconductor industry analyst explaining the state of the global chip ecosystem to an informed investor and technologist. No headers, bullets, stage directions, or 101-level definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: AI Products & Applications
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the most important new AI products, services, developer tools, enterprise platforms, applications, APIs, agents, models, and software released or meaningfully updated over the last few days.

Write for a sophisticated listener who already understands AI, software development, capital markets, enterprise technology, and the difference between impressive demos and durable products. Do not provide a generic AI news roundup. Lead with what changed at the margin: the product launch, feature release, API addition, pricing change, integration, enterprise deployment, developer capability, workflow improvement, or platform expansion that materially changes what users or businesses can actually accomplish.

Focus on products rather than companies. This briefing is not primarily about which frontier AI lab is winning or losing, nor about semiconductor competition. Instead, explain how new capabilities are changing real-world workflows, software categories, enterprise adoption, and consumer behavior. Mention competitive dynamics only where they help explain why a product matters.

Cover the following only where there is fresh, relevant information: consumer AI assistants, coding tools, research agents, productivity software, search products, enterprise copilots, document intelligence, multimodal applications, voice interfaces, browser agents, robotics software, creative tools, video generation, image generation, music generation, AI-powered office suites, customer service platforms, healthcare software, legal technology, finance applications, education tools, scientific software, cybersecurity products, autonomous agents, vertical AI companies, infrastructure software, developer SDKs, orchestration frameworks, evaluation tools, vector databases, MCP implementations, RAG platforms, observability products, workflow automation, open-source AI applications, and emerging startups from anywhere in the world.

Cover important new frontier or open-weight model releases only insofar as they enable new products or unlock new capabilities for developers and users. Do not spend time comparing model benchmark scores or analyzing competitive positioning among AI labs unless the product implications are significant. Likewise, discuss infrastructure products only where they materially improve deployment, inference cost, developer productivity, reliability, or enterprise adoption rather than the underlying semiconductor technology.

Prioritize practical capability over technical novelty. Explain what someone can now accomplish that was previously difficult, expensive, or impossible. Distinguish between meaningful improvements in workflow and incremental feature additions. Highlight products that reduce friction, eliminate manual work, improve quality, lower costs, expand accessibility, or create entirely new software categories.

Connect product launches to business implications. Explain what each development means for enterprise software, SaaS incumbents, consulting firms, cloud providers, software developers, knowledge workers, creators, small businesses, education, healthcare, financial services, and other industries being transformed by AI. Discuss where AI products appear to be replacing existing software versus augmenting existing workflows.

Be skeptical of marketing claims. Distinguish between demonstrations, limited previews, beta programs, generally available releases, and products with proven customer adoption. Flag where companies appear to be shipping genuine improvements versus rebranding existing functionality. Separate impressive technical demonstrations from products that are likely to achieve sustained real-world usage.

Pay particular attention to developer experience and ecosystem effects. Track new APIs, SDKs, plugins, protocol support such as MCP, integrations with existing enterprise software, pricing changes, open-source releases, licensing changes, workflow automation, interoperability, and tools that reduce the complexity of building or deploying AI-enabled software.

Be quantitative and specific: include release dates, pricing, subscription tiers, API costs, user counts, customer deployments, funding rounds where relevant, benchmark improvements only when they translate into practical capability, enterprise adoption figures, performance metrics, supported languages, context limits where applicable, and meaningful comparisons with previous versions. Cite the source of major figures in natural spoken language.

Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing primary sources first: company blogs, documentation, product release notes, GitHub repositories, developer documentation, API documentation, pricing pages, conference presentations, technical papers, and official announcements. Then use high-quality reporting and analysis from The Information, Stratechery, SemiAnalysis where relevant, Interconnects, Import AI, The Batch, Ars Technica, The Verge, Semafor Tech, TechCrunch, Bloomberg, FT, WSJ, and other credible technical and business publications.

The briefing should sound like a single experienced technology product analyst explaining what new AI products are genuinely changing how people work and build software. Focus on practical capability, product quality, and business impact rather than hype or company rivalries. No headers, bullets, stage directions, or 101-level definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: Enterprise Technology & Software
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the most important developments over the last few days across enterprise technology, cloud computing, software infrastructure, cybersecurity, developer platforms, SaaS, digital transformation, and the commercialization of artificial intelligence.

Write for a sophisticated listener who already understands enterprise software, cloud infrastructure, AI, capital markets, software architecture, and corporate IT. Do not provide a generic enterprise technology news roundup. Lead with what changed at the margin: the product launch, enterprise deployment, customer win, earnings signal, pricing change, acquisition, partnership, capital allocation decision, organizational shift, or technology adoption that materially changes the competitive landscape.

Focus on business execution rather than technological novelty. Explain how enterprises are actually adopting technology, where software budgets are flowing, which vendors are gaining or losing strategic relevance, and what the evidence suggests about long-term competitive positioning. Distinguish between product announcements, customer pilots, production deployments, meaningful revenue contribution, and durable platform adoption.

Cover the following only where there is fresh, relevant information: Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Google Cloud, Oracle, Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, IBM, Cisco, Dell, HPE, VMware, Red Hat, Snowflake, Databricks, MongoDB, Confluent, Elastic, Atlassian, GitHub, GitLab, Cloudflare, Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike, Zscaler, Fortinet, SentinelOne, Okta, Palantir, C3.ai, Adobe, Intuit, Workday, HubSpot, Zoom, Stripe, Figma, Notion, OpenAI Enterprise, Anthropic Enterprise, and other enterprise technology providers where relevant.

Cover cloud computing where there is meaningful new information regarding hyperscaler competition, cloud migration, AI infrastructure services, managed databases, enterprise networking, storage, developer platforms, observability, platform engineering, Kubernetes, containers, APIs, workflow automation, integration platforms, low-code development, identity management, data platforms, enterprise search, business intelligence, and infrastructure software.

Discuss AI primarily through the lens of enterprise adoption rather than frontier model competition. Explain how organizations are integrating AI into existing workflows, software platforms, customer service, software development, finance, healthcare, legal, manufacturing, logistics, cybersecurity, and knowledge work. Focus on measurable adoption, productivity gains, implementation challenges, governance, and return on investment rather than benchmark performance.

Connect technical developments to business outcomes. Explain what each development means for enterprise software spending, IT budgets, cloud consumption, operating margins, recurring revenue, customer retention, pricing power, implementation cycles, consulting demand, software valuations, and competitive dynamics. Highlight where incumbent vendors are successfully incorporating AI into existing platforms versus where startups are creating entirely new software categories.

Pay particular attention to the evolution of enterprise software architecture. Track the movement toward AI-native workflows, autonomous agents, workflow automation, MCP adoption, API ecosystems, open-source infrastructure, interoperability, developer productivity, and the changing role of software engineers, IT departments, and systems integrators. Explain where AI is replacing traditional software functionality and where it is simply becoming another feature.

Be skeptical of enterprise technology narratives. Distinguish between announced partnerships and actual customer deployments, pilot programs and production usage, bookings and recognized revenue, user counts and active engagement, proof-of-concept projects and organization-wide adoption. Flag where vendors appear to be rebranding existing capabilities as AI and where genuine platform shifts are occurring.

Be quantitative and specific: include dates, annual recurring revenue, cloud growth rates, customer counts, contract values, remaining performance obligations, operating margins, capital expenditures, subscription pricing, seat counts, deployment figures, adoption metrics, usage statistics, implementation timelines, acquisition values, and changes versus prior expectations. Cite the source of major figures in natural spoken language.

Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing primary sources first: company earnings calls, SEC filings, investor presentations, product documentation, technical blogs, conference presentations, pricing pages, developer documentation, customer case studies, and official announcements. Then use high-quality reporting and analysis from The Information, Stratechery, SemiAnalysis where relevant, Interconnects, Bloomberg, FT, WSJ, Reuters, CIO, InfoWorld, CRN, VentureBeat, Ars Technica, The Verge, Semafor Tech, TechCrunch, and other credible enterprise technology publications.

The briefing should sound like a single experienced enterprise technology strategist explaining how software, cloud computing, and AI are reshaping the modern enterprise. Focus on commercial execution, enterprise adoption, and long-term competitive positioning rather than product hype or headline-driven reporting. No headers, bullets, stage directions, or 101-level definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: The Throughline (daily synthesis)
prompts.prompt:
You are writing THE THROUGHLINE — the daily cross-briefing synthesis and the "front page" of the show. This is NOT a researched briefing; it is a synthesis of the OTHER briefings written today. Before writing, read every other enabled briefing script for today (the briefings/<id>.txt files, excluding this one) and write a single roughly 1000-word (about 6-7 minute) spoken-word synthesis that ties them together for a sophisticated listener.

Lead with the single biggest cross-domain story of the day — the one variable or theme that shows up across multiple briefings — and name it explicitly. Then develop three to four "throughlines": connections, shared drivers, or second-order links that cut across the individual briefings (for example, a geopolitical shock that simultaneously moves rates, energy, and crypto; a constraint that binds several domains at once; or value migrating across a stack). For each throughline, reference by name the specific briefings it draws from, so the listener knows which episodes to go deeper on.

Crucially, include a "where the briefings disagree" beat: surface a genuine tension or contradiction between the individual briefings (e.g. one briefing's optimism against another's caution). Exposing that tension is something no single-topic briefing can do and is the unique value of this segment. Close by pointing at the days and catalysts ahead that will resolve the open questions, clustering them where they cluster.

Do NOT do fresh web research or introduce facts not present in the day's other briefings — your job is to connect and elevate what was already reported, not to add new reporting. Write for the same expert audience and follow the editorial standard and format in CLAUDE.md: single narrator, conversational, no headers, bullets, or stage directions; open with a one-line greeting that names "The Throughline" and states the date; close with a one-line sign-off.

prompts.name: AI in Biopharma R&D
prompts.prompt:
Make a 1200 to 1500 word spoken-word briefing on the current state and most important recent developments in the use of artificial intelligence across the biopharmaceutical value chain, from early discovery through commercialization.

Write for a sophisticated listener who already understands drug development, capital markets, AI, and the difference between a compelling demo and a validated capability that survives contact with biology, the clinic, and regulators. Assume fluency in the R&D pipeline, trial design, regulatory pathways, and the economics of pharma; do not define basic terms. Lead with what changed at the margin: the model release, clinical readout, partnership, platform deal, regulatory signal, publication, or capital allocation move that actually shifts where and how AI creates value in drug development.

Map AI's contribution across the value chain and across functions, covering the following only where there is fresh, relevant information: target discovery, disease-pathway and mechanism identification, human genetics and functional genomics, network and systems biology, protein structure and molecule design (small molecule, antibody, and other modalities), PK/PD and translational modeling, biomarker discovery, CMC and process development, preclinical and IND-enabling studies, first-in-human and dose-finding, clinical study design and development planning, companion diagnostics, regulatory strategy and submissions, clinical operations, decentralized and digital trials, real-world evidence, intellectual property, and the commercial functions of sales, marketing, and medical affairs.

Explicitly address where the emphasis and capital sit today versus where the low-hanging fruit is going forward, and be specific about which parts of the stack are delivering measurable value now versus which remain promising but unproven. Distinguish clearly where large language models are the workhorse (literature synthesis, regulatory documents, trial-protocol drafting, coding, agents for operations) from where specialized architectures matter — computer vision for digital pathology, imaging biomarkers, and high-content screening; geometric and diffusion models for structure and molecule design; sequence and multi-omics foundation models for biology. Name which frontier models are most powerful for which tasks, where open-weight models are competitive and why, and whether sponsors are building proprietary in-house models — and if so, what trends are emerging around protecting the underlying IP, data, and model weights.

Connect developments to business and capital-markets implications. Explain what each means for large pharma R&D productivity, biotech platform companies, the AI-drug-discovery names, contract research and manufacturing organizations, diagnostics, cloud and compute providers, and the tools-and-instruments vendors. Be explicit about the through-line to R&D spend, pipeline economics, and the probability of improving clinical success rates.

Be skeptical of easy narratives. Distinguish between retrospective validation and prospective results, in-silico predictions and wet-lab confirmation, a molecule entering the clinic and a molecule succeeding in it. Flag where an 'AI-designed' claim is marketing over substance, where a platform's clinical track record is still too thin to judge, and where the binding constraint is data quality, biology, or trial timelines rather than model capability.

Be quantitative and specific: include dates, clinical phase and endpoint data, hit and success rates, timeline and cost reductions claimed versus demonstrated, deal values and milestones, funding rounds, model names and benchmarks where they translate into real capability, and deltas versus prior expectations. Cite the source of major figures in natural spoken language.

Use the freshest available sources at authoring time, prioritizing primary material first: company press releases and SEC filings, ClinicalTrials.gov, FDA and EMA guidance and decisions, peer-reviewed papers (Nature, Science, Cell, NEJM and their sister journals), preprints on bioRxiv and medRxiv, earnings calls, and conference presentations (JPM Healthcare, ASCO, AACR, NeurIPS/ICML biology tracks). Then use high-quality reporting and analysis from Endpoints News, STAT, Nature Biotechnology, BioPharma Dive, Fierce Biotech, Evaluate, and — for the model and compute layer — The Information, Stratechery, SemiAnalysis, Interconnects, and The Batch.

Trends in AI usage in the analysis of real world data. Can structured and unstructured data in the biotech be utilized to identify new indications, tractable eligibility criteria, confirm epidemiological estimates, and so on. Can the AIs access data across the balkanized digital formats that were adopted post HITECH and is HIPAA an impediment. What new technologies and companies are being used to take advantage of all the RWD out there? How can it improve the odds of success and lower R&D costs?

Close by pulling the threads together: the biggest open questions, the realistic potential to improve clinical success rates, and the likely impact on pharma R&D spend going forward.

The briefing should sound like a single sharp analyst who understands both drug development and frontier AI explaining where the field genuinely stands to an informed investor and scientist. No headers, bullets, stage directions, or 101-level definitions in the script itself. Open with a one-line greeting and the date. Close with a one-line sign-off.
